food_subgroup: Hard-boiled eggs in spiced Indian tomato-onion gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Indian Egg Curry. Nearest parent is a cooked egg in spiced sauce. A new concept for egg-based Indian curries is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hard-boiled eggs in spiced sauce, Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of egg curry is 2 eggs with gravy (approximately 200g total) — 2 hard-boiled eggs (halved or whole, often first shallow-fried to create a slightly crispy surface) in a medium-thick onion-tomato gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EX000023
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN under entry EX000023 — Egg Curry, standard Indian preparation with hard-boiled eggs in tomato-onion gravy.

gi_source: GI testing not applicable — Egg Curry contains low carbohydrates (6.5g/100g from gravy vegetables). It does not meaningfully raise blood glucose.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 636mg per 2-egg serving (200g) — elevated; the gravy and salt from cooking concentrate sodium significantly.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should reduce added salt in the gravy and limit portion size. The egg itself is a good source of potassium and magnesium for blood pressure support.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable. High protein (8.5g/100g) from eggs promotes satiety and blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg curry is an excellent choice for diabetic patients — low in carbohydrates, high in protein, and moderate in fat. Eggs provide a low GI protein source that does not raise blood glucose.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggs are one of the best dietary sources of selenium (22mcg/100g) and vitamin D (1.5mcg/100g) — both critical for immune function; turmeric provides curcumin.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the selenium and vitamin D from eggs support immune regulation and bone health. The turmeric in the gravy provides anti-inflammatory curcumin.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Curry is a widely enjoyed Indian dish — hard-boiled eggs (often first shallow-fried in oil to create a golden, slightly crispy surface) simmered in a medium-thick onion-tomato gravy spiced with cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala. It is a simple, economical, and nutritionally excellent curry available at virtually all South Indian and Indian restaurants in Singapore. Rich in high-quality protein, vitamins B2, B12, D, and selenium from the eggs, combined with the anti-inflammatory spices from the gravy.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under entry EX000023 — Egg Curry, standard Indian preparation.
notes: Contains eggs — not vegan. Suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians. Cholesterol is high (195mg/100g, approximately 390mg per 2-egg serving) from the egg yolks. GI and GL are null — negligible carbohydrate content. Vitamin D (1.5mcg/100g) makes egg curry one of the few non-fish Indian dishes with meaningful vitamin D content.
